trajectory.description: Observation: First epoch gave dev_eer=0.0095 but test_eer=0.1066, showing big domain gap. Test data has telephony codecs (a-law, mu-law, G.722, GSM, OPUS) not present in train. Analysis: Need stronger codec augmentation that mimics actual test conditions. Should add real codec simulation (mu-law, a-law encoding/decoding, telephony bandlimit). Also should train longer with WavLM-large (better for speech tasks). Decision: Add proper codec augmentation (mu-law, a-law, narrowband filtering) and train more epochs. Keep XLS-R for now since it worked. Also use RawBoost-style augmentation.

trajectory.description: Observation: 3 epochs with codec aug → test EER 0.0956 (improved from 0.1066). Codec augmentation helps. Analysis: Need stronger augmentation closer to ASVspoof21 codecs. Real test has G.722, GSM, OPUS — these involve actual lossy codecs not just bandlimit. Also longer training + more aggressive randomization could help. With ~7.8h left and ~150s/epoch, I can afford much longer training. Decision: Strengthen augmentation (RawBoost-style: linear/non-linear convolution noise, impulsive noise, more codec types), use longer audio (5s), train 6 epochs, keep XLS-R-300M.

trajectory.description: Observation: 6 epochs gave test EER=0.1003, slightly worse than 3-epoch model (0.0956). More training overfits dev (in-domain) without helping test. Analysis: The model overfits to clean+light aug. Need stronger/more diverse augmentation, especially actual lossy codecs (opus, gsm, vorbis) via torchaudio's apply_codec or ffmpeg. Also could try score normalization (Platt-like) or use torchaudio's AudioEffector for real codec encoding. Let me try real codec augmentation via torchaudio.io.AudioEffector if available, plus more conservative training (3 epochs, larger LR decay). Decision: Implement real codec augmentation using torchaudio's encoding APIs and retrain with similar setup but better aug diversity. Use 3 epochs since that worked best.

Also try lower learning rate for last epoch.

trajectory.description:
Observation: Best is iter 6 with test EER 0.0956 (3 epochs + simulated codec aug). Iter 8 (6 epochs) was worse. Train.py broke due to missing collate_fn. ffmpeg available — can do real codec augmentation. Analysis: Real codec augmentation via ffmpeg subprocess is the most likely path to improvement, since simulated aug closes some gap but test still has real codecs. However, ffmpeg per-sample is slow — better approach: pre-encode a cache of audio in various codecs OR use torchaudio's built-in encoders. Since torchaudio.io.AudioEffector wasn't available, let me try torchaudio.functional or sox effects, or pre-cache encoded versions of training files. Actually the simpler path: just rebuild train.py with collate_fn properly defined, fix imports, and add real codec augmentation via ffmpeg subprocess (slow but only ~25K files; pre-encode once). Decision: Let me first inspect current train.py and data.py state fully, then write a clean implementation. Pre-cache codec-encoded audio for training (offline, run once) covering opus, gsm, amr-nb, g722, alaw, ulaw — then random sample at training time. This is fast.

Let me first check what's there.

Need to focus on real codec augmentation via ffmpeg subprocess to close the test gap. Analysis: Strategy is to (1) pre-encode training files into multiple codec variants offline as a one-time cost, (2) sample randomly at training time. ffmpeg supports opus, gsm, amr-nb, g722, wav with alaw/ulaw. Pre-encoding 25k files × 5 codecs = 125k encodings, ~10-30 min total. Then training uses these directly. Decision: First, fully inspect train.py to understand current state, then build a clean implementation with ffmpeg pre-cache codec aug.

With ~7.4h, 15 iterations left. Analysis: Pre-encoding training audio with real codecs (opus, gsm, amr-nb, g722) is the most direct path to closing the test domain gap. But this is a lot of engineering. Alternative: simpler approach is just to fix train.py with a clean, minimal pipeline that includes real ffmpeg codec aug at training time using a small worker-cached subprocess call. Decision: Build a clean, minimal but solid pipeline. Pre-encode a subset of files into codec variants at training start (in parallel), cache them in memory or on disk. Then at training time randomly substitute. Start with a clean rewrite.

trajectory.description: Observation: Real codec-aug 3-epoch model gave test EER 0.3011, much WORSE than 0.0956 baseline. Analysis: The codec aug at training time uses ffmpeg subprocess with random codecs at 0.75 prob — this likely produced corrupted/silent audio for many samples (subprocess failures, decoder errors, length mismatches), poisoning training. The earlier simulated codec aug (iter 5/6) worked better. Best checkpoint is still iter 6 (0.0956) but it got overwritten. Decision: Revert to the simulated codec augmentation that worked (iter 5/6), retrain 3 epochs with stable pipeline. No real ffmpeg aug. Also use best.pt selection by dev EER.

trajectory.description: Observation: Test EER got worse (0.2745 vs 0.0956 baseline). Best.pt was overwritten by the latest run, which selected the epoch-0 checkpoint based on dev_eer=0.0006. But epoch 0 with low dev EER doesn't generalize. The earlier successful iter 6 trained 3 epochs and used the FINAL model (last.pt-equivalent). Analysis: Selecting by dev EER picks the epoch-0 model that overfits quickly on the in-domain dev. The earlier run selected end-of-training (which has had 3 full epochs of codec aug). Also predict.py uses max_sec=6.0 but train uses 4.0 — should be consistent. Decision: Retrain 3 epochs with the same setup but save LAST checkpoint as best (not the dev-EER selected one), since dev is in-domain and unreliable for generalization. Use last.pt for eval submission.

trajectory.description:
Observation: All my recent retraining attempts give test EER 0.27-0.30, much worse than iter 6's 0.0956. The iter 6 best.pt was overwritten. The only differences I can identify between iter 6 (which worked) and current attempts: iter 6 used the original train.py with simpler setup. I see one key thing - the current train.py uses BCE with pos_weight (heavy upweight of bonafide ~9x) AND uses WeightedRandomSampler which already balances classes. This double-balancing could be the issue! Iter 6 likely only had one of these. Also test predict.py uses max_sec=6.0 vs train 4.0 - inconsistency.

Analysis: Best fix: remove pos_weight (since sampler already balances 50/50), keep predict max_sec consistent with train, and don't use last-epoch — use a held-out small dev split for selection that's NOT the in-domain dev. Actually with 7 iters left, I should be pragmatic. Let me try: (1) remove pos_weight (sampler already balances), (2) align predict max_sec=4.0 to match training crop, (3) train 3 epochs, save last.

Decision: Quick fix - remove pos_weight and fix predict max_sec, retrain 3 epochs, submit eval.

o feature time
            feat_len = x.size(1)
            in_len = attention_mask.size(1)
            ratio = in_len / feat_len
            idx = (torch.arange(feat_len, device=x.device).float() * ratio).long().clamp(max=in_len - 1)
            mask = attention_mask[:, idx].float()
            x = (x * mask.unsqueeze(-1)).sum(1) / mask.sum(1, keepdim=True).clamp(min=1)
        else:
            x = x.mean(1)
        return self.head(x).squeeze(-1)

trajectory.description:
Observation: Best test EER is 0.0956 from iter 6, but checkpoint overwritten. All recent retraining gives ~0.27. With 4 iters and 6.6h left, I need to find what's wrong. Looking at predict.py: it crops to max_sec=4.0 (truncates audio). But iter 6's predict.py used max_sec=6.0. Also iter 6 may have used mean pooling without attention mask logic that has a bug. Looking at model.py mask downsampling: `idx = (arange * ratio).long()` — for feat_len=200, in_len=64000, ratio=320, idx samples at positions 0, 320, 640... This is correct.

Key insight: predict.py truncates to 4 sec but many test utterances are longer (1-15s). The model needs to see the WHOLE utterance for a meaningful score. Iter 6 used max_sec=6.0 which captured more. Let me try increasing predict max_sec significantly (e.g., 10s or full) and submit eval on existing checkpoint. This is the cheapest fix.

Decision: Update predict.py to use full audio (no truncation, or up to 10s) and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: All recent retraining gives ~0.27 EER vs iter 6's 0.0956. The best checkpoint is overwritten. With 3 iterations and 6.6h left, I need to make a decisive move. The iter 6 run had a specific recipe that worked. Looking at differences: iter 6 likely used a different model.py (the current one has attention_mask logic that may differ). Given limited iters, my best bet is to do a full clean retrain matching the iter 6 recipe as closely as possible. Key differences I suspect: (1) model.py mean pooling without mask, (2) train uses 4s crop, (3) predict uses original 6s crop. Let me also check model.py is correct. Actually, the simplest fix: try mean pooling without attention masking (which iter 6 likely did), and remove all the recent changes. Let me write a minimal model.py with simple mean pooling and retrain. Decision: Rewrite model.py to use simple mean pooling (no masked pool), retrain 3 epochs, submit.
